title: On Pornography, Demons, and Rod Dreher

comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Well, that was unexpected...

Monday's regular "Magic Monday" ask-me-anything post here on my Dreamwidth account featured, among many other things, questions from a young man who was troubled about certain aspects of his habit of masturbating to pornography. He noted that it felt very much as though some being outside himself was pushing that habit on him. This is far from the first time I've heard the same thing from people in his position, and -- as I normally do on my Magic Monday posts -- I responded to him on the basis of classic occult philosophy, which holds that: 

a) malign spiritual entities exist, and can manipulate the minds of unwary human beings; 

b) human sexuality is not simply a physical process, but involves the subtle levels of reality as well;

c) certain sexual behaviors can, under some circumstances, attract malign spiritual entities, who feed on the subtle substances emitted during those behaviors. 

(I should probably note that I consider these statements to be true, not merely because they're part of classic occult philosophy, but because I've found consistently that they provide better explanations of the cosmos than other points of view.) 

So we had a lively and, I hope, constructive conversation about how to deal with the situation using standard magical practices, and I figured that was that. This afternoon, though, I found that it went a great deal further than that -- because Rod Dreher (that's him in the picture), a regular columnist for The American Conservative, had read the discussion and posted a long article of his own about it, titled "Porn Is Demonic, Says Top Occultist,"  and using it to push the idea that pornography ought to be banned. Of course the result was a fine donnybrook in the comments page, with Christians denouncing Dreher for citing an occultist, Neopagans insisting huffily that most Neopagans don't agree with me, a great deal of back and forth about Dreher's proposed remedy, and much more. 

Since Dreher included a link to my discussion in his article, I'd like to comment a little on his essay and a few of the comments on it -- not least because Dreher garbled what I said in some important ways. A good deal of that garbling came from the inevitable differences between the ways we see the world, but not all. 

So let's cover some of the basics. 

1. Is pornography demonic?  Strictly speaking, that's like asking how much virtue weighs; there's a category error involved. Pornography consists of images and texts -- that is, material things -- that are made by a great many people for a great many reasons, mostly but not entirely centering on a desire to make money. Do malign spirits encourage some people to make these things, and others to consume them? The latter, to judge from the comments I've fielded, seems to be true in some cases, and I'm not prepared to deny that both may be true in many cases. That doesn't justify insisting that every image of a person not wearing clothes is demonic. 

2. Is masturbation bad? Like alcohol, or any other source of pleasure, it's harmless to those who can be moderate about it and problematic to those who can't, and issues of addiction can get involved. Yes, malign spirits can involve themselves in that latter point. The terror of sexual pleasure that Christianity borrowed from late Greek philosophy has probably caused more human suffering than any other single bad idea in history -- but the opposite of one bad idea, you know, is typically another bad idea, and obsessive sexual addiction is no more healthy than obsessive sexual repression. 

3. Are the malign spirits I'm talking about demons?  Not in the Christian sense. A great deal of Christianity remains stuck in a quasi-dualist worldview in which every spiritual entity is either an angel or a demon -- that is, either a servant or an opponent of the Christian god. That impressively narrow view isn't the worldview of classic occultism (and it's not well supported by the data from human spiritual experience worldwide, either). To occultists, the cosmos is full of a vast profusion of spiritual beings, most of which are serenely uninterested in human beings and their concerns. In the cosmos, there are certain beings who, under some circumstances, prey on human beings. Are they all servants of One Big Bad Guy? No. As Eliphas Levi pointed out a long time ago, unity is a divine characteristic, and the most notable common factor of the patterns of behavior we call "evil" is that they conflict with one another. So instead of a Satanic "Lowerarchy," in C.S. Lewis' phrase, that end of the realm of spirits is a vast penumbra of vague, quarreling, dissentient beings pursuing their conflicting goals at each others' expense -- and occasionally at ours. 

Yes, I know Christians disagree with that. They're entitled to their opinion, even if it's wrong. 

4. What about the Pagans who insist I don't speak for all Pagans? I never claimed to do so. Most of the people these days who call themselves Pagans -- a term I don't use for myself, btw -- are Neopagans; they belong to a new religious movement that's been active in the Western world since the 1940s (though, in the usual way, it's backdated itself to the dawn of time). I don't belong to that movement. The Druid faith that's my spiritual home got started in the early 18th century; the late Isaac Bonewits, who did more than anyone else to publicize the label "Neopagan," spent much of his career saying nasty things about my kind of nature worship, denouncing it as "Mesopagan." The occultism that frames my philosophical and practical take on the issues we're discussing, in turn, began to emerge in the 19th century, drawing on material from the Renaissance and, through that, the late Classical era. Those are the traditions I draw on. I don't claim to speak for those, either, and neither can anyone else -- we don't do Popes, you know. 

5. Should pornography be banned? Er, and how well did that work in the past? Slapping a legal ban on pornography would simply provide organized crime with a new and lucrative cash flow. That will probably be very welcome to them, since they're losing the very substantial income they've gotten until recently via cannabis sales, but that's all it will do. It's not the job of governments to enforce moral virtue; it's the job of organized religion to teach and persuade individuals to embrace moral virtue. When you see religious spokespeople calling on governments to ban something for moral reasons, what you're seeing is a church admitting its own incompetence at one of its core jobs. 

Those are the points that come to mind at first glance. No doubt others will come up as the conversation unfolds. I would like it to be a conversation, for what it's worth. 

Update: I was pleased to note that Dreher has posted an update to his essay discussing my response in a typically thoughtful fashion. Of course he disagrees with me about core issues -- he's an Eastern Orthodox Christian, I'm a polytheist Druid occultist, I'm sure we would agree that the sky is blue but getting much past there might take some work -- but I consider it promising that the two of us can disagree civilly about issues that cause a lot of flying saliva in other contexts.

replies.title: Re: Christian persecution
replies.content:
1) You're assuming that eternal bliss in heaven and eternal torture in hell are the only options open to an omnipotent deity, which is rather a questionable assumption! Assuming that the Christian god is omnipotent, omniscient, and omnibenevolent, he could have set things up any way he wanted to; for example, he could have had souls reincarnate until they get it right. Even if he wanted to do the heaven-hell thing, he could have designed hell -- and human souls -- so that sooner or later even the most clueless soul will finally figure things out. Since the whole system was his invention from the get-go, there's no way to absolve him of moral responsibility for it.

2) As for prayer, is he capricious, is he indifferent, or is he limited? And why does every other religion on earth have roughly the same proportion of prayers answered to prayers denied as Christianity? From a polytheist standpoint -- in which there is no THE god, just lots of gods -- this makes perfect sense, but monotheist faiths that believe their god is omnipotent and omnibenevolent have to engage in a lot of special pleading.

3) The pants are a huge issue. I know a lot of people who aren't Christians any more because of that.

Orthodox give him a lot of side-eye. We do recognize him as a saint. We also tend to see his writings as really unfortunate. Like, if the West hadn't welded that stuff onto their theology, the east might still be on speaking terms with them.

In Orthodoxy, the question of Hell is... somewhat vague. Official stance seems to be "We don't know for sure, and dwelling on it is unhealthy." Don't quote me on that: I'm no theologian.

The most common view of hell I've encountered in the Eastern church is that hell and heaven are the same place: being in the direct presence of God. If you've spent your life valiantly and carefully attuning yourself to God, then finally being in His Presence is heaven. If you've spent your life unrepentant, contorting your soul into an ugly parody of the divine image it contains... then being in the presence of God is Hell.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No question, governments have become far too powerful and intrusive. As I see it, in a free society, the role of government consists of enforcing those minimum standards of behavior that are necessary for people to live together in relative peace, and providing those services that experience has shown are better handled by public officials rather than by private enterprise -- for example, police and fire protection.

But let's take it a step deeper. You're saying, here's a problem -- a lot of people are encountering pornography when they're very young and impressionable, and a case can be made that this is harmful to them. I don't argue with that for a moment. The fact that this is a real problem doesn't mean that passing a law to ban pornography is an effective solution. You need to address the solution as such, and show that it will actually solve the problem we're discussing.

In this case, we know better. Back when pornography was illegal in the US, it was still readily available, just as prohibiting drugs has done squat to decrease their availability. Slapping a legal prohibition on something without addressing the problem in any other way is not a solution. Do I have some other solution in mind? No, and that's exactly the point. What's needed is a serious collective conversation about the issue and a search for solutions that might actually solve something.

replies.title: the powers of demons
replies.content:
JMG, this is a general comment about the discussions we are having about demonic influence here and in the most recent entry about The Cosmic Doctrine on the other blog. My own outlook and practices are a mix but lean more toward Neopaganism than upon what you state is your background (classic Western occultism and the Druid Revival). I part company with many Neopagans and some witches in that I take the existence and activities of demons seriously. If one admits the existence of a variety of different spirit entities and does not have a view of the universe as inherently tending toward the Good as humans ordinarily understand it (i.e. what human beings desire or find beneficial), one sees no a priori reason to disbelieve in demons.

I don't worry about demons much because they don't seem to have affected me much before I took up magic, and the sorts of magic I choose to do doesn't engage with them or provide much energy for them to feed on. I take some basic precautions to keep them away. I do see a lot going on in the world that is difficult to explain without recourse to some kind of spiritual explanation such as demons, or the alternate explanation that an angry God is teaching us a lesson, which I reject.

I have a general problem, though, with statements or explanations that demonic forces are inspiring people to certain behaviors, encouraging them to persist them, or are the source of various misfortunes in our lives. It is not that I think that explanation is nonsense.

In our present state of knowledge, it seems unfalsifiable. Or at best, that to determine whether evil spirits are at work in a given situation, we have to consult experts, and when ordinary intelligent people have to rely on expert advice in a field so specialized that they have no way of checking the advice or the qualifications of the experts, it usually leads to trouble.

We have had or currently have this problem in urban planning, law enforcement, and in nearly every field related to medicine, to name but a few.

A related problem is that so many phenomena and experiences can be accounted for by the activities of demons that this idea lends itself to being a shortcut that stops people from investigating other causes. Belief in demons certainly seems to have functioned that way in some societies that believe in them.

I expect that you have a devastating answer to my reservations.

replies.content:
Interestingly, I was meditating yesterday on the idea of movement creating movement and thinking about it in terms of both the personal and political. The idea came to mind--and I suppose this isn't overly revelatory, but it was a specific form of thinking about it I had not yet done--that creating law absent a certain threshold of already-conforming behavior is likely a recipe for failure. The way I am thinking of it, the law itself does nothing to create tracks of movement for people to align with; its power can only reside in its ability to codify or create that behavior, which then helps spread that behavior through increased routing along such movement in space. Worse yet, if you create a law that runs counter to what most people are doing without first at least establishing a deep enough track for others to follow, you're likely to face serious blowback. In Fortune's metaphor, the movement isn't there.

You need to start with a concerted effort to move people away from overuse or unhealthy use of pornography, which is something that has to start at the individual and moral/ethical level, not at the legal level. Surely the most successful laws are going to be those that encode behavior that society has already engaged in and come to a more or less collective decision that it is positive enough to be deemed overly beneficial to the group. Granted, that's a messy process that can often take a long time, but creating legal frameworks in opposition to mass behavior is like trying to layer abstract rationality over instinctive behavior evolved over thousands or millions of years; good luck with the abstract rationality!

Of course, this then raises the question of how do you successfully create new movements in space that conflict against ingrained routes. Saying you have to change behavior before law is well and good--I think that's true--but that means you again need something more motivating than law to create the change in behavior. So what is it that can be done at the personal, religious, etc level that is more powerful than encoded law? Or, more specifically, in what ways does the former prism offer more promising tools than the latter?

This is something I perhaps should spend today's meditation on. But I wonder if it gets down to harnessing the same function of movement begetting movement, but using oppositional movement instead? One other clarity that came to me during my meditation that movement does not just beget movement of the same kind, but it begets oppositional movement, as well--not to mention presumably less binary forms of movement. So is there some innate capacity to harness or tap into those oppositional movements that belongs to us at the personal human level, at the individual level, that cannot be transferred to abstract legal code?

I suppose the most obvious there is looping back to the religious level and the capability to interact with divine beings and access other planes, among other routes. Hmmm. Lots to think on here . . .

replies.title: public safety and demonology
replies.content:
I presume our host will not mind my responding to your question 5), because the extent to which it is the State's responsibility to foster virtue is a perennial one.

The public safety argument was one of the main justifications for state and church sponsored witch hunting in the Renaissance and early modern period. The witch hunts resulted in the judicial murder of tens of thousands of mostly innocent people, destroyed their families, exacerbated social discord, were a handy excuse for all sorts of score settling, and were a huge waste of resources, since most of the crimes which they attempted to stamp out were either wholly imaginary or victimless. The sporadic witch trials that had taken place earlier in the Middle Ages had slightly more social justification, since there were higher bars to prosecution and at least some of the accused were people who made a habit of intimidating their neighbors by cursing them.

If you are going to regulate or criminalize activities which are alleged to feed demons, there are going to be social costs just as there are with any other kind of government regulation, including the expense of enforcement, the restriction on people's freedom to pursue happiness, and the opportunities for public and private corruption. I read in the morning newspaper that some local jurisdictions are considering making it illegal to use a vaporizer to ingest tobacco and cannabis in public, even though there is no direct health danger to others from secondhand smoke and the practice is not noisy or disruptive of the peace. The justification offered was that teenagers, who are not allowed to purchase vapong products, are getting hold of them anyway, and making it illegal to vape in public would cut down on teen vaping (which is unhealthy for the teenagers). These laws, if they are passed, are going to be a perfect opportunity for selective harassment, arrest and prosecution by law enforcement.

Governments have many other ways of promoting the spiritual welfare of the public. I would suggest gun control (not abolition); funding public parks and nature areas within walking distance; free admission to museums, classical music concerts, libraries and education; public monuments intended to inculcate virtues; continuing the tax exemptions and First Amendment protections for religious organizations; honors and awards for public service. It would also be immensely helpful if architects and landscape architects were trained in sacred geometry and feng shui.

comments.title: sex-porn-etc.
comments.content:
A number of years ago the sex advice writer, Dr. Ruth Westheimer co-authored a book with a rabbi in which they discussed their findings that many people, including clergy, do not know or understand their particular religion's position on certain sexual topics. While the Roman Catholic church has always been pretty clear, there are other churches that just don't talk about such things. The story of Onan is an example--according to Jewish interpretation he wasn't punished for wasting (spilling) his seed through masturbation--he was punished for coitus interruptus (pulling out) thereby denying his brother's widow her right to a child by a male of her dead husband's family--usually a brother. This custom was intended to prevent widows from being left without a set of relatives to support them in old age. Onan was being greedy, trying not to split his potential inheritance with a child which would be considered his nephew although biologically his son. But the whole tale about violating your dead brother's rights has been turned into a cautionary tale against masturbation.

There is a world of difference between the attitude that human sexuality is an extremely powerful force that can either unite people or tear societies apart and that it must therefore, like explosives, be regulated and guarded for the good of all vs. the attitude that human sexuality is a nasty distraction from the important things in life that should be constrained to the greatest extent consistent with continuing the human race. Every society that I can think of has some regulation of sexual relationships--but the kind and extent and enforcement of such regulations varies.

I agree that the types of pornography that are so readily available are probably harmful for young minds--no young man needs to be given the impression that his potential partners should be treated as mere receptacles, nor should young women be given the impression that they should tolerate such treatment. But at least a partial antidote would be accurate and fully available education about sexuality--the sex paranoia of the Christian right makes this impossible in the US. I have read quite a bit by Dan Savage, the gay sex advice columnist who started the "It Gets Better" campaign. He talks of how, as a gay man, it was impossible for him to try to educate minors without being suspected as a predator grooming potential partners. Even to say to young gays--"been there, felt that"--simply wasn't possible until the internet made it possible to communicate to young people privately and from a distance. I think this is true of well-intentioned adults in general.

Rita

comments.title: Opposites and extremes

But they seem like two very different questions. As I’ve understood this conversation over the past months of Magic Mondays, the conversation on porn has been heavily linked to a broader conversation on passive media use in a broader sense, with porn being particularly dangerous in that

A: porn consumption is by its very nature passive, nobody in the world I’ve ever heard of engages their higher faculties while consuming it despite this hilarious attempt at suggesting its possible to make porn that somehow does just that (https://amp.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2012/may/22/alain-de-botton-porn-ideas). Passivity leads to unfiltered receptivity, which in and of itself is dangerous.

B: Porn, unlike other passive internet uses like say... cat videos... has an etheric component because it typically involves masturbation, which releases etheric energy and therefore combines passive unfiltered receptivity with etheric residues that are more likely to attract parasitic entities.

C: Due purely to our culture’s lingering sexual repression, as discussed above, porn tends to come wrapped in all of society’s suppressed urgent and taboos... and so even the most vanilla of tastes have to sort through content laden with simulated sexual violence, racism, rage, misogyny and the like (since Hate is the New Sex the first place it finds itself ruling is the world of porn) - which means you’re combining passive receptivity, the etheric equivalent of putting blood in the water in shark infested waters, and everything our society represses including all its hate and rage in one place. All of which makes a recipe for disaster.

The attempts to wrap it in shame and repression by certain fundamentalists seems to lead to self hate, sexual confusion, and life-long complexes for everyone who goes through adolescence in that environment. I know you’ve mentioned before in several of your books that it’s really only a good idea magically if you are practicing a tradition that specifically uses and directs those energies. Plenty of us on the left realize the class dimension and excesses of the Woke Movement. I've written on that many times, and it is an active matter of contention within Neopagan circles. It has contributed to the implosion of some of our biggest and oldest festivals. There are a lot of aspects of Wokeness which are absurd. It is full of privileged white people forever and dramatically offended "on behalf" of minorities who never appointed them as representatives and who often have much more nuanced views on the subjects at hand. There are shrill activists trolling for any "microaggression" they can turn up to start a row over.

That does not change the fact that there are real grievances and social justice problems at the root of things, and it does not, for most of us Pagans, justify any sort of alliance with Christians whose cure is worse than the disease any day of the week.

comments.title: Holy Water, Florida Water and Natural Magic
comments.content:
If I may;

The subject of cold water purification inspired me to write a little instructional on making holy water on my blog: https://violetcabra.dreamwidth.org/4651.html, which I can imagine might be quite relevant. I know in some traditions, washing the genitals -- along with other crucial energy centers in the body -- with holy water is a standard daily practice.

This makes me wonder the possibilities of using other sacred substances for ritual purification. For instance, if someone were masturbating excessively to pornography I wonder if dousing the genitals liberally with Florida water and washing their computer screen with Florida Water would provide any help -- if I may, I'm curious your thoughts on the theoretical merits of this, JMG?

Also, I wonder if any one who has struggled with this has tried the red-bag amulet tied around the waist with a little bow, so that the bag rests against the lower dantian? My understanding is this is where etheric energy tends to move from and to. As such, specifically protecting this point, the point targeted by malicious spirits engaged in energetic siphoning of sexual energy, seems appropriate. It's a bit of an awkward spot given the fashions in clothing....perhaps a red bag amulet could be put into the pouch of a hoodie?

replies.content: Thanks for this.  It would be worth experimenting with Florida water, and with other protective substances. As for the red bag, I think you'd want to put it rather lower on the body -- I'd expect parasitic entities who feed on sexual energy to draw it through the genital center rather than the womb center. Still, experimentation is called for.

replies.title: Re: Holy Water, Florida Water and Natural Magic
replies.content:
You're very welcome! Okay, my sense of occult anatomy was a bit hazy -- now it's a bit more refined -- thank you.

Given the many ways that having a red bag amulet in the underwear is awkward -- I can't help but imagine the scene in which it falls out during work in front of one's most distinguished colleagues! -- Having it in the underwear unsecured also means having it roll about which 1) could make sitting painful; 2) could cause it to come into proximity with the anus which could cause it to become rather foul rather quickly

I think that the problems of placement may make it a no go. I'll go even further, I think that most sorts of ways of securing any type of amulet in proximity to a male member would likely have big problems with either blood flow, pinching, poking, or simply falling out.

For this reason of pragmatics I think that some sort of protective substance would be more indicated; the Holy Water formulation I discuss above is one idea, Florida Water is another, and another are the many protective oils sold at Lucky Mojo -- Cast Off Evil Oil seems appropriate; Protection Oil equally so; as does Run Devil Run Oil given the nature of this discussion. Of course Fiery Wall of Protection is well-renowned, I wonder though, how fiery it is!

Another idea is to wash the clothing and body -- especially the underwear -- with protective herbs. Here I think Eucalyptus would be especially well-indicated from the Hoodoo tradition: Eucalyptus is used specifically to help break bad habits, to remove pestiferous influences from one's life, and to protect one for one's own personal weaknesses, catherine yronwode gives a great description here: http://www.herbmagic.com/eucalyptus.html

This single herb is perhaps the most theoretically indicated used as a simple; it seems totally feasible that one could have a bottle of Dr. Bronner's Eucalyptus Castile Soap and use it in both the shower and to launder clothing. It's cheap -- less than $20 from the company for a 32 oz bottle: https://shop.drbronner.com/pure-castile-liquid-soap#scent=Eucalyptus; readily available at many supermarkets; and already formulated to be at a safe concentration, and it is highly fragrant even when used as a laundry detergent and even after the clothing comes out of the dryer. For this reason, I think that it could be effectively used and circumvent most of the problems I could foresee.
